agent_output: {"executive_summary":"The logistics platform needs a unified API gateway layer to replace the current fragmented approach where each service independently handles auth and rate limiting. The design introduces a single logical gateway with three distinct ingress channels (mobile, partner, internal), centralized authentication, tiered rate limiting, and strict network isolation. This eliminates inconsistent auth implementations, reduces cross-cutting concern duplication, and creates a clear security boundary between external and internal traffic.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"API Gateway (Edge)","responsibility":"Single entry point for all external traffic. Terminates TLS, performs initial request validation (size limits, malformed payloads), routes to the correct authentication pipeline based on ingress channel, enforces rate limits, and forwards authenticated requests to backend services with a standardized identity header.","boundaries":"Sits in the DMZ. Handles ONLY cross-cutting concerns: auth, rate limiting, request validation, routing, observability headers. Contains ZERO business logic."},{"name":"Mobile Auth Pipeline","responsibility":"Validates JWT/OAuth2 bearer tokens for mobile consumers. Extracts user identity, checks token expiry, validates scopes. Attaches X-Consumer-Id and X-Consumer-Type: mobile headers to downstream requests.","boundaries":"Plugin/filter within the gateway. Only processes requests arriving on the mobile ingress route prefix (/api/mobile/v*)."},{"name":"Partner Auth Pipeline","responsibility":"Validates API keys for B2B partners. Looks up partner identity from a key store, checks key status (active/revoked/expired), validates allowed IP ranges if configured. Attaches X-Consumer-Id, X-Consumer-Type: partner, and X-Partner-Tier headers.","boundaries":"Plugin/filter within the gateway. Only processes requests arriving on the partner ingress route prefix (/api/partner/v*)."},{"name":"Rate Limiter","responsibility":"Enforces per-consumer rate limits using a sliding window algorithm backed by Redis. Mobile: 100 req/min keyed by user ID. Partner: 1,000 req/min keyed by partner ID. Returns 429 with Retry-After header when exceeded. Emits rate-limit metrics.","boundaries":"Runs after authentication (needs consumer identity to key the limit). Shared Redis instance for distributed counting across gateway replicas."},{"name":"Internal Service Mesh Gateway","responsibility":"Handles service-to-service traffic on an internal-only network. No authentication but enforces mTLS between services for transport security. Applies internal rate limits (high ceiling, circuit-breaker style) to prevent cascading failures.","boundaries":"Deployed on a private network/VPC with no public ingress. Completely separate listener from the external gateway — no route from external traffic can reach this."},{"name":"Webhook Dispatcher","responsibility":"Manages webhook subscriptions for B2B partners. Receives domain events from backend services, formats payloads per partner contract, delivers with retry logic (exponential backoff, 3 retries), signs payloads with HMAC for partner verification.","boundaries":"Async component. Consumes from an event bus/queue. Does NOT sit in the request path. Partners register webhooks via the partner API."},{"name":"Backend Services","responsibility":"Core logistics domain services (orders, tracking, fleet, etc.). Stripped of all auth and rate-limiting logic — they trust the gateway's identity headers. Focus purely on business logic.","boundaries":"Only reachable from the gateway or internal mesh. Never exposed to public internet. Validate X-Consumer-Type header presence as a safety check."},{"name":"Gateway Config Store","responsibility":"Stores route definitions, rate limit tiers, partner API keys, CORS policies, and webhook subscriptions. Provides dynamic configuration without gateway restarts.","boundaries":"Internal datastore (Redis/etcd/Postgres). Read-heavy, written to via admin API only."}],"interfaces":[{"from":"Mobile App","to":"API Gateway (Edge)","contract":"HTTPS with Bearer JWT token in Authorization header. Route prefix /api/mobile/v1/*. Response includes X-RateLimit-Remaining and X-RateLimit-Reset headers.","coupling_type":"loose — versioned REST API with content negotiation"},{"from":"Partner Systems","to":"API Gateway (Edge)","contract":"HTTPS with X-API-Key header. Route prefix /api/partner/v1/*. Response includes rate limit headers. Webhook registration via POST /api/partner/v1/webhooks.","coupling_type":"loose — versioned REST API with published OpenAPI spec"},{"from":"Internal Services","to":"Internal Service Mesh Gateway","contract":"mTLS gRPC or HTTP on private network. Service discovery via DNS. No auth tokens — identity established by mTLS certificate CN.","coupling_type":"platform — tightly coupled to internal infrastructure but loosely coupled between services"},{"from":"API Gateway (Edge)","to":"Backend Services","contract":"HTTP with mandatory headers: X-Consumer-Id, X-Consumer-Type, X-Request-Id, X-Forwarded-For. Gateway strips all upstream identity headers before forwarding to prevent spoofing.","coupling_type":"loose — gateway and services communicate via standardized headers, services are unaware of gateway implementation"},{"from":"Backend Services","to":"Webhook Dispatcher","contract":"Domain events published to message bus (e.g., order.shipped, delivery.completed). Webhook Dispatcher subscribes and transforms events to partner-specific webhook payloads.","coupling_type":"decoupled — async event-driven, no direct dependency"},{"from":"Rate Limiter","to":"Redis","contract":"INCR/EXPIRE commands on keys formatted as ratelimit:{consumer_type}:{consumer_id}:{window}. Sliding window with sub-second precision.","coupling_type":"infrastructure — operational dependency on Redis availability"}],"data_flow":"External request → TLS termination at Gateway → Route matching determines consumer type → Auth pipeline validates credentials and extracts identity → Rate limiter checks/increments counter in Redis → Request forwarded to backend service with identity headers → Backend processes request → Response flows back through gateway (no transformation, gateway is transparent on egress) → Rate limit headers injected into response. For webhooks: Backend emits domain event to message bus → Webhook Dispatcher consumes → Looks up partner subscriptions → Delivers signed payload to partner endpoint with retry.","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"API Gateway / Edge Server","rationale":"Centralizes cross-cutting concerns (auth, rate limiting, routing) that are currently duplicated inconsistently across services. Single point to enforce security policy."},{"pattern":"Strangler Fig (migration)","rationale":"Services currently handle their own auth. Migrate incrementally: route traffic through gateway, remove auth from services one at a time. No big-bang cutover."},{"pattern":"Backend for Frontend (BFF) variant","rationale":"Separate route prefixes for mobile vs partner act as lightweight BFFs — each gets tailored auth, rate limits, and potentially different response shapes without separate deployments."},{"pattern":"Sliding Window Rate Limiting","rationale":"More accurate than fixed-window counters (avoids burst-at-boundary problem). Redis-backed for distributed consistency across gateway replicas."},{"pattern":"Event-Driven Webhook Delivery","rationale":"Decouples webhook delivery from the request path. Services don't need to know about webhooks — they emit domain events, and the dispatcher handles delivery and retries independently."},{"pattern":"Header-Based Identity Propagation","rationale":"Gateway authenticates once and propagates identity via trusted headers. Backend services never handle credentials directly — simpler, more secure, easier to audit."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"Gateway / Edge","classification":"generic","aggregates":["Route","RateLimitPolicy","ConsumerIdentity"]},{"name":"Partner Management","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["Partner","ApiKey","WebhookSubscription"]},{"name":"Identity & Access","classification":"generic","aggregates":["User","Token","Session"]},{"name":"Logistics Core","classification":"core","aggregates":["Order","Shipment","Fleet","Tracking"]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Identity & Access","downstream":"Gateway / Edge","relationship":"Open Host Service — Gateway calls IAM to validate tokens via a standard protocol (OAuth2/OIDC). Gateway conforms to IAM's token format."},{"upstream":"Partner Management","downstream":"Gateway / Edge","relationship":"Customer-Supplier — Partner Management provides API key registry and webhook config. Gateway consumes it. Partner Management team accommodates Gateway's query needs."},{"upstream":"Logistics Core","downstream":"Webhook Dispatcher","relationship":"Published Language — Core publishes domain events in a documented schema. Webhook Dispatcher transforms them to partner-specific payloads via an ACL."}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Single logical gateway with route-based consumer separation (not three separate gateways)","gains":"Simpler operations — one deployment, one config surface, shared infrastructure. Easier to maintain consistent security policies.","sacrifices":"Blast radius: a gateway bug or misconfiguration affects all consumer types. Noisy-neighbor risk between mobile and partner traffic.","alternatives_considered":["Three separate gateway deployments (one per consumer type) — better isolation but 3x operational burden and config drift risk","Service mesh only (no gateway) — works for internal but doesn't solve external auth centralization"]},{"decision":"Redis-backed sliding window rate limiting","gains":"Accurate rate limiting across distributed gateway replicas. Sub-second precision. Well-understood operational model.","sacrifices":"Redis becomes a critical dependency — if Redis is down, rate limiting fails. Must decide on fail-open vs fail-closed.","alternatives_considered":["Local in-memory rate limiting — no external dependency but inaccurate with multiple gateway replicas","Token bucket algorithm — simpler but less precise for the stated per-minute windows"]},{"decision":"Async webhook delivery via event bus (not synchronous from gateway)","gains":"Webhook delivery doesn't block or slow API responses. Natural retry semantics. Backend services stay decoupled from partner concerns.","sacrifices":"Eventual consistency — webhooks may be delayed. Adds operational complexity (message bus infrastructure). Harder to debug delivery failures.","alternatives_considered":["Synchronous webhook delivery from gateway — simpler but adds latency to API responses and couples gateway to partner endpoint availability","Polling API instead of webhooks — simpler to build but poor partner experience and higher load"]},{"decision":"Strip auth logic from backend services (gateway handles all auth)","gains":"Single source of truth for auth policy. Services are simpler. Easier security audits.","sacrifices":"Gateway becomes a single point of failure for auth. If bypassed (misconfigured network), services are unprotected.","alternatives_considered":["Defense in depth — services also validate tokens — more secure but duplicates logic and the inconsistency problem persists","Zero-trust per-service auth — maximum security but significant complexity and latency overhead"]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Rate limit tiers","evolution_path":"Rate limits are stored in config, not code. Can introduce new tiers (premium partners, internal tools) by adding config entries. Can evolve to dynamic rate limiting based on load."},{"area":"Authentication methods","evolution_path":"Auth pipelines are pluggable filters. Can add OAuth2 PKCE for mobile, mutual TLS for premium partners, or SAML for enterprise customers without changing gateway core."},{"area":"Route prefixes and API versioning","evolution_path":"Versioned route prefixes (/v1/, /v2/) allow running multiple API versions simultaneously. Can deprecate versions by updating gateway config."},{"area":"Webhook event types","evolution_path":"New domain events automatically become available for webhook delivery. Partners subscribe to event types they care about. Adding new events requires zero webhook infrastructure changes."},{"area":"Gateway implementation","evolution_path":"Architecture is implementation-agnostic. Can start with Kong/AWS API Gateway/Envoy and swap later. The patterns (auth pipelines, rate limit keys, identity headers) transfer across implementations."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"X-Consumer-Id / X-Consumer-Type header contract","reason":"Every backend service depends on these headers for authorization decisions. Changing the header format or names requires coordinated changes across all services. Lock this contract down early with a schema."},{"area":"Network isolation between external and internal traffic","reason":"Internal services MUST NOT be reachable from the external gateway's network. This is a security invariant that must be enforced at the infrastructure level (VPC/firewall rules), not application level. Changing this would compromise the entire security model."},{"area":"Rate limit key structure","reason":"Changing how rate limit keys are constructed (e.g., from per-user to per-user-per-endpoint) requires careful migration to avoid resetting all counters and potentially allowing burst traffic during transition."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Gateway becomes single point of failure — all external traffic flows through it","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Deploy multiple gateway replicas behind a load balancer. Health checks with automatic failover. Rate limiter fails OPEN (allow traffic) if Redis is unavailable — prefer availability over rate enforcement. Circuit breakers on backend calls."},{"risk":"Migration period: some services still do their own auth while gateway also does auth, causing double-auth or auth conflicts","severity":"high","mitigation":"Use Strangler Fig pattern. During migration, gateway sets a X-Auth-Handled: true header. Services check this header — if present, skip local auth. Remove local auth code only after gateway route is verified. Track migration progress per service."},{"risk":"Redis failure degrades rate limiting across all consumers","severity":"high","mitigation":"Redis Sentinel or Cluster for HA. Local in-memory fallback rate limiter (less accurate but functional). Alert on Redis connectivity issues. Rate limiter fails open with aggressive monitoring."},{"risk":"Partner API key compromise — no second factor","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Support IP allowlisting per partner. API key rotation without downtime (support multiple active keys per partner). Anomaly detection on usage patterns. Webhook signing with separate HMAC secret."},{"risk":"Header spoofing — external request injects X-Consumer-Id header to impersonate another user","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Gateway MUST strip all X-Consumer-* and X-Request-* headers from incoming requests before processing. Only gateway-injected headers reach backend services. This is non-negotiable — verify with integration tests."},{"risk":"Webhook delivery failures cause unbounded queue growth","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Dead letter queue after max retries. Per-partner circuit breaker — if a partner's endpoint is consistently failing, pause delivery and alert. Retention policy on the event bus (e.g., 7 days max)."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Phase 1: Deploy gateway with passthrough routing (no auth, no rate limiting) to establish traffic flow and validate routing. Phase 2: Enable auth on gateway — mobile JWT validation first (highest traffic, most inconsistent current implementations). Use X-Auth-Handled header for gradual migration. Phase 3: Enable rate limiting with generous initial limits, tune based on observed traffic. Phase 4: Migrate partner API key auth to gateway, implement webhook dispatcher. Phase 5: Remove all auth code from backend services once all routes go through gateway. Use an existing gateway product (Kong, AWS API Gateway, Envoy + ext_authz) — do NOT build from scratch.","files_to_create_or_modify":["infra/gateway/gateway.yaml — Main gateway configuration (routes, plugins, upstreams)","infra/gateway/rate-limiting.yaml — Rate limit policies per consumer type","infra/gateway/auth-mobile.yaml — JWT/OAuth2 plugin configuration","infra/gateway/auth-partner.yaml — API key validation plugin configuration","infra/gateway/cors.yaml — CORS policies per route prefix","services/shared/middleware/strip-auth.ts — Middleware to remove local auth when X-Auth-Handled is present (migration aid)","services/webhook-dispatcher/ — New service: event consumer, subscription manager, delivery engine","infra/redis/rate-limit-cluster.yaml — Redis deployment for rate limiting","docs/adr/001-api-gateway-adoption.md — Architecture Decision Record"],"patterns_to_follow":"Use declarative gateway configuration (YAML/HCL) over imperative code. Rate limit policies should be data-driven from the config store, not hardcoded. Auth pipelines should be composable filters — never nest auth logic. All gateway-to-service communication must include X-Request-Id for distributed tracing. Webhook dispatcher should be idempotent — duplicate event delivery must not cause duplicate webhook calls.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Do NOT put business logic in the gateway — it handles auth, rate limiting, routing, and nothing else. Business rules belong in services.","Do NOT build a custom gateway from scratch — use battle-tested products. The differentiation is in your logistics domain, not in HTTP proxying.","Do NOT use a shared database between gateway and backend services — gateway config is its own datastore.","Do NOT implement rate limiting with fixed windows — use sliding window to prevent burst-at-boundary attacks.","Do NOT skip header stripping — this is the most common API gateway security vulnerability. Test it explicitly.","Do NOT deploy without a kill switch — ability to bypass gateway and route directly to services in an emergency (with VPN/bastion access only)."]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":88,"high_confidence_areas":["Three-channel ingress with route-based separation is the right model for these consumer types","Centralizing auth at the gateway eliminates the inconsistency problem definitively","Redis-backed sliding window rate limiting is the industry standard approach for this scale","Header-based identity propagation is well-proven and simple","Async webhook delivery via event bus is the correct decoupling strategy"],"low_confidence_areas":["Specific gateway product choice (Kong vs Envoy vs cloud-native) depends on existing infrastructure and team expertise — not enough context to recommend","Exact Redis topology (Sentinel vs Cluster) depends on scale and existing Redis infrastructure","Whether mTLS is already in place for internal services — if not, adding it is a significant infrastructure project"],"assumptions":["The platform runs in a cloud environment with VPC/network isolation capabilities","The team has or can operate Redis infrastructure","Traffic volume is moderate enough that a single logical gateway (multi-replica) can handle all three consumer types without resource contention","An existing identity provider (OAuth2/OIDC) exists for mobile user authentication","The team prefers using an off-the-shelf gateway product over building custom"]}}
agent_output_excerpt: {"executive_summary":"The logistics platform needs a unified API gateway layer to replace the current fragmented approach where each service independently handles auth and rate limiting. The design introduces a single logical gateway with three distinct ingress channels (mobile, partner, internal), centralized authentication, tiered rate limiting, and strict network isolation.
